items.title: OpenAI rolls out ‘Codex for (almost) everything’ (new Codex capabilities beyond coding)
items.why_it_matters: Signals Codex expanding into broader agentic work (not just code), which could change how teams automate knowledge work inside OpenAI’s ecosystem.
items.brief: Codex expands beyond coding into broader task support (per OpenAI’s announcement post).
items.details: OpenAI posted an announcement describing ‘Codex for (almost) everything,’ positioning Codex as more than a coding assistant. The post highlights broader task coverage and workflow support beyond writing code. This is a meaningful product-direction signal even if some features roll out progressively. If you rely on Codex, expect more end-to-end task handling rather than isolated code generation.

items.title: Google: Gemini 3.1 Flash TTS (new expressive text-to-speech model)
items.why_it_matters: High-quality, low-latency TTS is a key building block for real-time voice agents; new model options can reduce cost and improve naturalness.
items.brief: Google introduced Gemini 3.1 Flash TTS, aiming at more expressive, next-gen AI speech.
items.details: Google announced Gemini 3.1 Flash TTS as a next-generation text-to-speech model. The positioning emphasizes expressiveness and improved speech quality for AI applications. For builders, this is most relevant for voice agents, call-center automation, and multimodal assistants where latency and prosody matter. Watch for API availability/pricing details and supported languages/voices in the rollout.

items.title: Google Chrome: ‘Skills’ turns prompts into one-click tools
items.why_it_matters: This is a practical path from “prompt” to reusable UI—useful for teams standardizing repeatable AI workflows without building full extensions.
items.brief: Chrome adds ‘Skills’ to package your best prompts into one-click tools.
items.details: Google introduced ‘Skills’ in Chrome, framing it as a way to convert prompts into reusable, one-click tools. This targets everyday productivity: repeatable transformations, drafting, summarization, and lightweight automations directly in the browser. For teams, it’s a distribution mechanism for vetted prompts and workflows with less friction than sharing docs. The key question is how Skills integrates with Gemini and what governance/admin controls exist for orgs.
